raw_payload.author: ljfrank
raw_payload.content:
The movie had me in tears of joy when it started (yes I am THAT fan). I understood the plot better than Duke. The movie format suited the plot points but did not suit the motivation(s) behind it.

The key to Peaky Blinders wasn't the plot but the characters. They were each fully formed and flawed so you understood the why the character acted in a certain way (ex;:Arthur's marriage to Linda).

But this movie seemed like a a series's season condensed into a 2 hour format. I think that it would have been better as a full season to explore the dynamics.

The heart of PB was its ability to provide the characters as damaged humans. Here some of the series characters were mentioned then tossed aside. That said I will rewatch it on the small screen. By order of the Peaky Blinders.

raw_payload.author: CallumGrimesx
raw_payload.content:
Attended the early red carpet premiere in Birmingham ✅

Tommy Shelby is back blessing our screens once more! It follows the show and creates some high stakes involving World War Two and the Nazis, it shows Birmingham in that time and how vulnerable it was in its state of decay from Germany and the bombings and paints a true historical picture of that period that educates and honours those who lived through it. Tommy returns and is a tormented soul who struggles deeply which you can feel, he has lots of emotional moments that are rare for Tommy but make this stand out. Having him be with his son and try to reconcile that complex relationship is engaging with a legacy for Tommy which gets a lot of serious moments of them trying to connect in some way. Tommy is a badass in this, he is older but that doesn't stop him from doing some crazy cool stuff. The Nazis and Tommy and gang fighting each other has some amazing action packed thrilling scenes that feel like they have real high stakes. I like how focused this is on family and healing the Shelby name through many sad moments new and old from the show from the greats we've lost. The ending possibly solidifies the end of the peaky blinders story and it's going to be a bittersweet one but it's a great and solid ending to the characters and story we love.

Attending this premiere was absolutely awesome! A great beautiful venue and seeing the cast attend the screening was wonderful.

Cillian is great returning as Tommy and displays the torment he holds in him really well aswell as being a badass once more. Barry Keoghan is great as the son of Tommy taking up the mantle with difficulty and a youthful personality. Tim Roth as the bad guy is awesome, he is manipulative and calculated and yeah he's just really cool anyway as an actor.

The soundtrack was a very key player in the film. Lots of music and songs throughout and I love what they did with the iconic song from the show and tweaked it a bit to fit the mood.

Some great cinematography in this that elevates these emotional and pivotal character moments the film gives us.

Peaky Blinders was peak once more!

raw_payload.author: fozia-52252
raw_payload.content: Top tier movie. The movie is about the new generation of peaky blinders coming in and how the old generation comes to an end. Every question you had throughout the movie is answered and it's the perfect ending to Tommy Shelby. He leaves a legacy and the character development in such a short amount of time is insane. Awesome film, no notes. The movie was enjoyable throughout with gripping scenes from the start with a nod to some iconic historical images (exactly what you want from a period drama - you'll know it when you see it). Soundtrack was great and the story moved at such a well thought out pace without being too long. You don't have to be a die hard Peaky Blinders fan to love this movie, but I would recommend you read up on Arthur Shelby so you don't get a little lost in some minor parts.

raw_payload.author: niged-701-928132
raw_payload.content: A weak plot, weak sub-plots and a cast bereft of Alfie Solomon and the past ones that made the series so good. It hasn't transported to the big screen well and has lost many of the qualities that made the series a classic. I'm genuinely baffled how others have rated it so highly - I'm a fan but not blind to the film's limitations.

raw_payload.author: Mikheil-H
raw_payload.content:
The Peaky Blinders have always been a mixed bag for me. With one series being outrageously forgettable, to another series being one of the greatest in all of television, it was always going to be interesting to see where The Immortal Man would sit.

The Immortal Man acts as a swan song for the Shelby Family Saga, focusing on Tommy returning to Birmingham for a "one last job" type exploit.

Cillian Murphy is the stand out. Immediately slipping back into the role as elegantly as his trench coat flaps down the street. He brings more spiritual nuance and melancholy to the "gipsy king" than has been seen previously, but have no fear, the "aura farming" is quick to return. With a sequence on a horse to go down in infamy on YouTube Shorts I'm sure.

Tragically, though Barry Keoghan and Rebecca Ferguson try their best, notable absences from the original show's cast permeate, leaving the film feeling like a distinctly separate entity. (Though research shows, the absence of particular actors was unavoidable).

It's hard to tell what the lasting impact of "The Immortal Man" will be. I am potentially in a minority who believed the final moments of series 6 were a perfect send off for Tommy Shelby. Regardless, The Peaky Blinders has left a strong cultural legacy, and this finale of sorts does not damage that. A statement that is becoming all to rare for many shows these days!

raw_payload.author: EmG-22
raw_payload.content:
We were so looking forward this, the 6 series of peaky were AMAZING, I new deep down what the outcome would be, but I don't know, one minute I think i loved it but then felt disappointed, just something missing for me, didn't quite have me on the edge of my seat.

I really think Arthur should of been kept in!

raw_payload.author: a-clinch1
raw_payload.content: I am a fan of the series, but truth be told, I felt the series went on for one season to long. I tell you this, so that if you disagree then my score will not be something that you will relate to. This film felt like a love letter to the series but lacked much substance. The first half was incredibly slow and the second half never picked up that much. Laboured would be my word for the second half. Cinematography was excellent but the story was average with no real tension. A film that won't last in the memory.

raw_payload.author: RetaliatedB
raw_payload.content: This film was everything I wanted to see. The old peaky blinders characters came to an end and the new generation began. Duke Shelby is now the new gipsy king of the Peaky Blinders. And it will be interesting to see him take his role on now. Thomas Shelby gets a well written ending that was long over due since the series finale back in 2022. The characters are very well written, and it's quite an emotional roller coaster so be prepared for some heart break when watching this. The opening sequence to this film just screams peaky blinders so from there you Knew you were in for a treat. Overall rating 8-10.

raw_payload.author: stefan-61722
raw_payload.content:
Not very contingent, the plot is shaky, with so many scenes either inconsistent with the characters natures, or very spontaneous/ unexplained.

However, the few good scenes there were, were all both memorable and emotionally loaded, so it's still very much worth the watch for those.

Overall 6/10, could've been so much more though.

raw_payload.author: S4StatusOfficial
raw_payload.content:
I watched this movie yesterday and ended up seeing the whole show more than once. I've never experienced something so intense in a theater before. Even after the movie finished, we stayed back talking about it with other people-it was that incredible.

The story takes place a few years after the last season and is basically split into two parts. The first part introduces the new characters while also showing where Tommy is in his life. His mental state at the beginning feels very true to how Season 6 ended.

Then the second part shifts gears and becomes pure action. It's nonstop and completely wild. The emotions hit hard-I laughed, I cried, and in some moments the suspense was so strong I almost forgot to breathe. Honestly, I would have loved if the movie lasted another hour.

The writing is brilliant, the direction is flawless, and the music is bold, seductive, and perfectly matched to the scenes. The performances are fantastic-Barry does a great job, and Cillian is extraordinary as always. At this point, it feels like there's no limit to his talent. And seeing that level of cinematography on the big screen was simply amazing. 🎬✨

raw_payload.author: jeremybradley-49651
raw_payload.content:
This was easily my most anticipated film of the year and it didn't let me down at all.

Great storyline, acting and visuals, thoroughly recommended for existing and new fans of the series.

Overheard someone say on the way out that they are going to watch the series now, obviously a new fan!

Cillian Murphy was born to play Thomas Shelby.

raw_payload.author: lsimonsulzer
raw_payload.content:
No credible script, no Arthur (who was 1/2 the interest golf the Peaky Focking Blinders), no real emotion, characters coming from nowhere (Tommy's « real » son like the first didn't exist).

Only the photography can vaguely redeem that movie which we could call « more money for us ».

But the photo is amazing. Port resemble Turner's paintings. The camera angle is great.

raw_payload.author: RyanC-142
raw_payload.content:
Being immersed in the Blinders world felt like connecting with an old friend again, except it's 1940 now a leap from 1919 Birmingham streets of season 1 all the way to the shadow of WWII Great Britain. The movie hooks you in right from the beginning, compelling and exactly what you'd expect from the Shelby family

Binging Peaky Blinders for the first time with my roommates back in 2020 was such a delight and some of those feelings came back while viewing this flick. Cillian Murphy brings his 'A' game as always being the suave cold calculating anti-hero! I ended up chuckling to myself because you know what's gonna happen to other characters when they talk to Thomas Shelby with even the littlest iota of disrespect...Uh-Oh!

As always its a delight to see Tim Roth - (Reservoir Dogs/ Hateful 8) and Rebecca Ferguson-(Mission Impossible/ Silo ) held their own with such grace and decorum but Barry Keoghan ( Saltburn/ Banshees of Inisherin) astonished me without a doubt! His portrayal of Shelby's son, Duke, carries a chilling unpredictability (the family blood) that only Barry Keoghan can deliver! I had a reservation or two as to the impact of the overall story so I can't give it a complete perfect score. All in all, I would give it a 7.5 out of 10. Oi! Let's round it to 8!

Peaky Blinders: The Immortal Man comes to Netflix on March 20th and when the day arrives, PUT CAPTIONS ON, not as a recommendation but a necessity! SLAINTE!

raw_payload.author: andyfilm-38035
raw_payload.content:
Nothing about this film is in any way inspired or interesting. Throughout the entire screening I kept thinking of how much better the 4 leads (Murphy, Ferguson, Keoghan, Roth) were than the material they were given to work with. Long drawn-out melodramatic scenes that we've seen a million times before.

The Netflix formula is becoming really tedious and transparent. They can take any sort of creative leaps they want and they so often revert to keeping it perfectly safe and within the boundaries of the formula.

I'm going to go cleanse my palate with something from A24, Janus or NEON.

raw_payload.author: annie-186
raw_payload.content:
I went into this film hoping for a gripping story and a good cinematic experience, and it absolutely delivered. The pacing is a slow build, but in the best possible way. The tension gradually tightens scene by scene, drawing you deeper into the story until you're completely invested. Rather than relying on constant action, the film takes its time developing atmosphere, characters, and stakes, which makes the payoff far more satisfying.

The performances across the cast are excellent. The acting feels grounded and believable, and the characters are portrayed with a level of nuance that keeps you engaged throughout. There's a quiet intensity to many of the scenes that reminds me of the storytelling style used in the TV series. If you enjoy that kind of layered drama where mood, dialogue, and character presence carry as much weight as plot, you'll likely appreciate this film.

Visually, it's also a treat. The cinematography is striking without being over-the-top, creating a strong sense of place and atmosphere that suits the story perfectly. Every frame feels carefully considered, adding to the immersive experience.

One of the standout elements for me was the music. The dramatic score sounded incredible through the cinema sound system and elevated many of the key moments. It adds tension, emotion, and scale in a way that really enhances the storytelling.

I watched this with a friend who hadn't seen the TV series, while I'm a big fan of that series, and we both thoroughly enjoyed it. That's probably the best recommendation I can give: it works equally well for newcomers and for viewers who already appreciate this style of storytelling.

Overall, a compelling, atmospheric film that rewards patience and delivers a memorable cinema experience. Definitely worth watching on the big screen.

raw_payload.author: michelle_siasat
raw_payload.content:
Epic and seamless send off to the main character. Very clever plot.

Did not feel like 2 hours. It could have been another season. I would have liked to have seen Tommy Shelby old, grey and playing with grandkids. The story was so poetic. Great choice of villain.

9/10. One point deducted because Lizzie was not included in the narrative at all.

All actors on point. I didn't love Rebecca Ferguson (I usually love her in everything). But she seemed out of place in this genre and the Birmingham world.

Set design with the pigs and horses were a work of art. Cillian really shines. Ada's final suit was exquisite. *chefs kiss*

A film all fans will be satisfied with.

I hope deleted scenes will be available on Netflix.

raw_payload.author: TwoNonCriticsAndACat
raw_payload.content: I went to watch this movie yesterday. I watched the entire show multiple times. I've never had such a visceral experience in a theatre. After the movie ended we stayed talking about with people, it was absolutely incredible. It picks up some years after the last season ended and it's kind of divided in two parts: the first is a bit more introductory of the new characters and we get to see Tommy and how he's doing. Tommys state of mind at the beginning is completely coherent with end of season 6. The second part is action action action. OMG! It was wild! A rollercoaster of emotions, I cried, I laughed, I didn't breath in some scenes from the suspense. Honestly I would have wanted another hour 😂 Phenomenal writing, impeccable direction, the music is wild, sexy and so fitting, the acting is phenomenal: Barry was really good and Cillian is stellar as always, there no longer a limit to this man's talent and what a joy to be able to see that cinematography in a movie theatre! Amazing!

raw_payload.author: bdnvctm
raw_payload.content:
The new Peaky Blinders film arrives with the weight of enormous expectations, and for the most part it delivers a brooding, cinematic continuation of the Shelby saga. Expanding beyond the intimate scale of the television series, the movie feels bigger, darker, and more reflective, treating its characters less like gangsters and more like myths forged by war, power, and regret.

At its center is Tommy Shelby, still magnetic and haunted, navigating a world that has grown more dangerous and politically volatile. The film leans heavily into atmosphere: long shadows, cold streets, and quiet rooms where every conversation feels like a negotiation for survival. The direction favors patience over spectacle, allowing tension to simmer rather than explode. When violence does arrive, it lands with brutal finality. Performances across the board are excellent, especially in the way familiar faces reveal the cost of years spent chasing control.

Yet the film isn't flawless. Its ambition occasionally stretches the narrative, and a few subplots feel underdeveloped compared with the emotional weight carried by Tommy's central journey. Some viewers may also miss the tighter episodic rhythm that once defined the story. Still, the movie succeeds where it matters most: it deepens the legend while giving its characters space to confront what they have become. By the time the final moments arrive, the tone is less about victory than legacy. It asks whether power can ever truly satisfy the people who sacrifice everything to obtain it.

raw_payload.author: fmmmjfhq
raw_payload.content:
Wow, never left a theatre with that many people sat in their seats speechless, for that alone it's one that must be seen on the big screen.

Cillian is, as always, a star. They managed to tie some complex family struggles, fascism, past war trauma and current war nicely together to make it easily understandable with a good flow.

Some really iconic scenes, particularly the one in The Garrison, classic Peaky Blinders.

We find out about characters past who don't star in the film, some more depth around certain characters, particularly Charlie would have been a welcome addition.

Overall, a classic that can be rewatched over and over. A dark tale, and not an easy watch, but peaky blinders at its best.

raw_payload.author: spectroniser
raw_payload.content:
I won't say much just so I don't let out any accidental spoilers but all I'll say is WOW.

Awesome movie with a fantastic cast, Cillian Murphy delivers again (shocker) and is supported by a very talented cast. I was kept excited the whole time watching, it had me on the edge of my seat. To keep my review impartial as always, the only critique I'd have is a couple of characters I'd liked on the show didn't make an appearance but it's not something that bothered me in the end as the cast in this movie delivers a stellar performance.

raw_payload.author: alisoncampbell-53316
raw_payload.content:
Spoilers incoming!

Went to watch the film today and was truly disappointed they did Arther Shelby dirty. The way he got written out no way Tommy would kill his brother. I know the actor in real life having some personal issues but soo many better ways he could have been written out.

We never find out what happened to Finn? We know he betrayed his brothers and told to leave but no revenge plot? Not know what he go up to?

Gutted no Esme and Lizzie cameo Jermaih son not in it, not even in back ground like you see him in episode. I thought maybe his ambition to be bigger than what he was in organisation would been a factor maybe?

No mention of mawsley even with fascists been a motive in movie still.

Just abit meh like it was a good film but predictable. You know going in to it would be like a goodbye piece to the Thomas Shelby I wish was more interesting, substance just miss the mark for me personally.

raw_payload.author: KayLemon
raw_payload.content:
I was a 13 year old boy when I sat down and watched the marvel that is Peaky Blinders. Today, at the age of 23, I returned home from seeing the immortal man on the big screen. Tommy is a character that I have held close to my heart for a decade and watching his story finally end was rather emotional but I believe it was handled well and executed just as accordingly.

From the way the film is shot to the score, this film is a wonderfully crafted piece of story telling. My only gripe is the way they handled the character of Arthur but seeing as the actor has lost the plot a little, I can see that their hands were tied slightly.

The man suffered immensely. He always yearned for death despite slipping through the reapers fingers on every occasion. In the end, he got what he wanted and what he thought he deserved but he died stopping both the nazi regimes plans and also stopped the thuggish future of his son.

I will miss seeing Cillian adorn the Tommy character and I will miss the peaky blinders.

In the bleak midwinter.

raw_payload.author: JacksonRDancer
raw_payload.content: This is my first ever review for a film on IMDB and it's going to Peaky Blinders: The Immortal Man, although as we know now, not so immortal. As a fan extending over five years, and one of my two favourite TV Show characters, the other being Rick Grimes, I am deeply saddened that he has died to the hands of his son, however it was soon coming, and after the death of his sister, who would he truly have left. I haven't cried in the cinemas as much as I did watching Tommy die since watching Interstellar 12 years ago. I'm proud of what this show has led to, I'm happy with the ending, but feel like a piece of me has broken apart now that the character is over, and that Cillian will also be moving on as well. Looking forward to what the universe has in store for the future, hopefully they keep Barry Keoghan playing Duke.

raw_payload.author: MeganB-708
raw_payload.content:
It was a good ending to a show however... the first 45 mins could have been cut in half. The role of the gypsy was a bit meh... Barry Keoghan was excellent by the end and dare I say nearly took away from Cillian's performance.

However with the trio of Fontaines, Keoghan and Cillian it felt a little "Irish is having a moment". I was half expecting Paul Mescal to appear.

The story line was interesting and the last hour was good and felt like true peaky blinders.

End scene was very good!

raw_payload.author: J-Dog26
raw_payload.content:
First of all.. I want to say that the visuals of this movie were incredibly well done especially with the dark tone Peaky Blinders always had.

Cillian Murphy being brilliant as always, giving us that chilling vibe of aura. Seeing him put the iconic suit and hat with the pocket watch was what I wanted to see.

Now.... Ada's death was so tragic, and the fact that it cut to Tommy feeling it and seeing her in the road was such a gut wrenching thing to see. Ada was definitely the last moral anchor he had.

Tommy's entrance to the garrison was so good. I mean.. pulling a grenade was so dirty and genius. Also him riding the horse in dried mud through the streets was visually stunning.

I initially thought Barry Keoghan would have been Charles. But I'm so glad he was Duke. He gave off that same kind of attitude the Duke had in the show. I like how it showed him potentially betraying his dad sharing information but ultimately staying loyal to him.

The third act of the movie was Tommy and the gang taking on Beckett and destroying the weapon that would win the war eg. The money.

Ultimately with Tommy blowing it up and killing him, whilst taking 2 bullets to the gut and duke saving him from the car. Amazing.

Finally with what I knew was coming was the end of Tommy Shelby with a bullet with his name on to do it. "In the bleak midwinter". So poetic with the funeral style the family were given.

Overall I thought it was a fitting ending to such a monumental character and Tommy Shelby will always be on top..

raw_payload.author: seppzn
raw_payload.content:
I honestly cannot understand why this movie was made. The original Peaky Blinders series ended on such a strong note, with rich characters, political tension, and one of the most compelling protagonists in modern television. This film manages to throw almost all of that away.

Where were the characters that actually made the show what it was? So many important figures are simply absent or irrelevant. The story barely feels connected to the world of Peaky Blinders at all. The show was always about family, power, strategy, and the psychological depth of Tommy Shelby. Here, none of that is explored in any meaningful way.

The plot feels rushed, confused, and strangely empty. Instead of building on the fascinating direction the series had taken - Tommy in politics, his influence growing, the consequences of his past - we get a storyline that feels random and poorly constructed.

And the character of Tommy Shelby himself feels completely off. This is supposed to be the man who outsmarted politicians, gangsters, and enemies for years. Yet in this film, he feels like a shadow of the character we followed for six seasons.

It almost reminds me of the disappointment many fans felt with the final season of Game of Thrones: enormous potential, beloved characters, and somehow it all collapses into something that feels careless and unnecessary.

Honestly, the most frustrating part is that it did not have to be this way. The world of Peaky Blinders had so many possible stories left to tell. Instead, we got something that feels like a hollow afterthought.

Sometimes the best decision is to leave a great story alone.

raw_payload.author: Miguel1226
raw_payload.content:
The Immortal Man continues the story of Thomas Shelby in a way that stays true to the tone and style of the original series.

Cillian Murphy delivers another strong performance, and the film maintains the dark atmosphere and sharp dialogue that fans expect from Peaky Blinders. The story works well as a continuation of Tommy's character arc and provides meaningful moments for returning characters.

However, the pacing occasionally slows the momentum, preventing the movie from reaching the intensity that defined some of the show's best episodes.

Overall, it's a strong character-driven continuation that fans of the series will appreciate.

raw_payload.author: PaigeC-298
raw_payload.content:
The movie was enjoyable and educates about a moment of history that is not well known.

With the ambiguous end of the series the first half does a good job of painting how Tommy had changed and withdrawn.

There are some funny moments.

Overall not as strong as the series, but with the limit on time and some actors not being able to take part I still enjoyed the goodbye to this beloved character.

It felt like a good end to the series.

raw_payload.author: Jacob-5937
raw_payload.content: Completely shatters what we know from the show. Despite polly in season 6 having visions saying tommy wont die to a bullet, he dies to a bullet. They murder the brotherhood between the shelby brothers at the snap of their fingers. They undid 6 YEARS of gypsy curses and fortunes by killing tommy the way they did. This doesnt feel like character assassination, it feels like universe assassination, all personality from the show ripped out and thrown on the big screen for shock value, probably to bring people into that sort of thing to the show. Instead of drug testing paul anderson, maybe we should start testing the writers.

raw_payload.author: mikeraybone
raw_payload.content: Just a great movie. I love the fact they are supporting independent cinema with the release too. It's tough wrapping up 6 series in a film to provide a springboard to the forthcoming 2 series. Steven Knight does a brilliant job. Cillian Murphy, Rebecca Ferguson, Barry Keoghan, Stephen Graham are some of the greatest actors around at the moment and they are superb. The mixture of Clint Eastwood in the spaghetti westerns, The Corleone's and Tommy's war damaged hero is genius. The ending is perfect, ultimately a flawed hero deserving of immortality.

raw_payload.author: macca-00836
raw_payload.content:
No spoilers here.

The Immortal man doesn't do well as a stand alone film you need to be a fan of the Peaky blinders series to watch it.

I would say it's abit rushed but the acting, music and the filming at top class. Definitely with seen it in the big screen.

It's interesting how it has so many 9 and 10 ratings but also 1-3s .

My advice is go see it and try to avoid any spoilers and make your own mind up. Give it a solid 7.

raw_payload.author: ldh-31427
raw_payload.content:
This film was fantastic and took all the loyal fans down memory lane. I would have loved to see this storyline play out through a full season because 2 hours wasn't enough. Cillian was brilliant as Tommy as always.

Where I take issue with the script is how much it neglected to wrap up lose ends from the show. Not one mention of Finn, even though the series finale led us to believe there would be some future show down with Duke. Tommy is understandably still distraught about Ruby but gives little thought to his son with Grace, the love of his life. Arthur was 1000% missed and the explanation of his death felt out of character for the two brothers.

It was amazing seeing old faithfuls like Johnny Dogs, Charley Strong & Curly. And of course the presence of Polly's spirit was constant. Would have liked to see development of Ada's son Karl and how he felt about her murder. And where did Isiah go?

Overall, if you are a fan of Tommy Shelby you'll love this movie but if you're a fan of the supporting cast, you'll be disappointed.

raw_payload.author: KarinaT-18
raw_payload.content:
After watching this movie I was left with a feeling akin to that of the last few episodes of GoT. The bar had been so set high through the incredible series and something felt so flat in this movie - starting mere minutes in.

There was so much that I feel this could have been, even with many of the key things and people that were left out.

I can only say that it didn't have the original charm of the Peaky Blinders series and it didn't answer any of the questions left to be answered which led me to believe that the things that had come next would've been better left a mystery.

raw_payload.author: michaelh-868
raw_payload.content: Movie is very good in my personal opinion, the only flaw for me was that the movie was not long enough, i would've loved to see a bit more of duke and how he achieved his status, although it was a great movie, very emotional ending, but at the end of the day it is exactly what tommy wanted which makes it a better. 10/10.

raw_payload.author: tridentfilms
raw_payload.content: Since coming back from Flanders the Peaky brothers have always been dead men walking, one by one they have met their maker until now there is just one left, Tommy Shelby. I've been watching him and his life story for over ten years and got quite emotional as the film ended and Tommy's story came to a final conclusion, an end that has always been predicted from the very start, we thought he achieved that end in the last TV episode, only for Ruby to come back and save him, and this time it was left to his eldest child to finally conclude his story, but only of course, by order of the Peaky Blinders.

raw_payload.author: LouisM-478
raw_payload.content: One of my favourite movies ever. The story setting in WW2 was perfect and the plot was very well structured. It was Peaky at its best. The ending was extremely sad but made for an amazing finish to one of the greatest series ever. I gave it a ten for good reasons. The soundtrack was superb and the acting was brilliant!

raw_payload.author: Jhbrown13
raw_payload.content:
Immortal Man: Comprehensive Spoiler Review

Review: Peaky Blinders: The Immortal Man

For fans who spent years immersed in the world of Peaky Blinders, the film continuation carries enormous expectations... but I consciously chose not to have any. The series built its reputation on meticulous character development, slow-burning narrative tension, and emotional weight that accumulated across seasons. Unfortunately, the film, while beautifully shot and strongly acted, feels like a story that was meant to be told across an entire season but was instead compressed into a two-hour film.

As a longtime fan, the result lands around a 5/10. Technically, the film is impressive. The cinematography remains stylish and atmospheric, the score captures the familiar tone of the show, and Cillian Murphy once again delivers a quality performance as Thomas Shelby. But beneath the visual polish lies a narrative that feels rushed, structurally uneven, and at times, fundamentally out of alignment with the characters that the series spent six seasons carefully building.

A Season's Worth of Story in Two Hours

The most obvious issue is pacing. Peaky Blinders thrived because it allowed events to unfold gradually. Political conspiracies, betrayals, family fractures, and psychological spirals were given room to breathe.

Here, the movie plays like an entire Season 7 storyline jammed into less than two hours. Major developments arrive abruptly, resolve quickly, and rarely have the emotional buildup they would have had in the series format.

Instead of the layered storytelling the show was known for, the film often feels like a highlight reel of plot beats

Creative Choices That Betray the Characters and The Story

More problematic than the pacing are the creative decisions that seem to contradict the core identity of the characters.

One of the most shocking examples is Tommy murdering Arthur. For a series built heavily on the fractured but unbreakable bond between the Shelby brothers, this decision feels profoundly out of character. The relationship between Arthur and Tommy was one of the emotional anchors of the show: violent, complicated, and often dysfunctional, but rooted in a deep familial loyalty forged in war and survival.

To resolve that relationship through fratricide feels less like a tragic inevitability and more like a narrative shortcut designed for shock value. Instead of feeling like the culmination of years of character development, it feels abrupt and thematically inconsistent with what the show spent six seasons establishing.

Another baffling decision is Tommy's choice to effectively allow the Nazis to rise to power without obstruction. The series had long portrayed Tommy as someone deeply entangled in political maneuvering and power structures. Watching him ultimately step aside as that historical tide rises feels strangely passive for a character defined by strategic intervention and ruthless agency.

Even if the intention was to show exhaustion or resignation, the film never properly builds the psychological bridge to that decision.

The Abandonment of His Sons

Perhaps the most emotionally confusing aspect of the film is Tommy's abandonment of his sons.

Throughout the series, much of Tommy's ruthless ambition was framed as an attempt to secure a future for his family. His children were supposed to represent the possibility of escaping the violence and trauma that defined the Shelby world.

For him to ultimately walk away from them without the emotional exploration that such a decision demands feels hollow. The film treats this moment as a quiet conclusion, but without the narrative groundwork it comes across less like a tragic resolution and more like an unresolved character contradiction.

Season 6 Deviation

The most obvious casualty of the narrative choice is the shift away from the unresolved tension surrounding Oswald Mosley and Nazism. Season 6 carefully positioned Mosley not just as a political antagonist, but as the embodiment of a larger ideological threat. The narrative was building toward a confrontation either ideological, strategic, or personal, between Tommy's world and the rising tide of fascism Mosley represented.

For fans who saw Season 6 as the beginning of Tommy Shelby's final transformation, the movie's direction feels less like a continuation and more like a narrative reset that ignores the groundwork already laid just so that it can have a quick, definitive ending.

Ada's Death: Abrupt and Unnecessary

The handling of Ada Shelby is another example of the film's rushed storytelling.

Her death arrives suddenly and without the emotional buildup the character deserved. Ada had evolved throughout the series into one of the Shelby family's most interesting figures-politically aware, morally grounded, and often the voice of reason within the chaos.

In the film, however, her death feels jarring and strangely unnecessary. Instead of serving as a meaningful turning point for the story or the characters, it comes across as a plot device that exists primarily to raise the stakes quickly. For a character who played such a significant role in the series, the lack of emotional weight surrounding her exit feels like a disservice.

The Emotional Disconnect

Perhaps the most disappointing aspect of the film is how little it ultimately made me feel.

The television series excelled at creating moments that were devastating, haunting, or triumphant. Even the quieter scenes carried emotional gravity because the audience had spent years living with these characters.

Despite strong performances, the movie never fully captures that depth. Key moments-Arthur's death, Ada's death, Tommy abandoning his sons-should have been emotionally shattering. Instead, they often feel strangely muted and underwhelming, as if the story is moving too quickly to allow the audience to process them.

The Question That Lingers

By the time the credits roll, one question hangs over the entire experience:

Why wasn't this just a final season?

Nearly every major problem with the plot itself: rushed pacing, underdeveloped emotional beats, abrupt character decisions... would likely have been solved by telling this story across six episodes instead of two hours.

Season 6 still feels like the true ending of the series. The film plays more like an extended epilogue that never fully justifies its existence.

Final Verdict

Peaky Blinders: The Immortal Man is visually impressive and well acted, but the storytelling choices ultimately undermine the characters and themes that made the series so powerful.

By compressing what feels like an entire season into a single movie and by making creative decisions that conflict with the characters' established identities the film struggles to deliver the emotional impact fans were hoping for.

There are moments that are enjoyable and worth appreciating, but overall the experience leaves a strange sense of detachment and even for someone who went in without heavy expectations, real disappointment. I would rather they left it alone than make this particular conclusion/continuation.

And honestly, it probably doesn't need to be seen in a theater

Again, my rating: 5/10.

raw_payload.author: ScottY-432
raw_payload.content: What a ride!!! For over a decade we have dressed like them, imitated their accent and counted down the hours to when the new seasons will hit our screens. Starting in the dirty, nasty streets of Birmingham as out right gangsters and capturing an entire audience who's loyalty was tested when politics and a slower pace finished the small screen story. But this... this was for everyone that stayed loyal. The immortal man, synonymous in so many ways now as he always will be in his own way in the world of cinema. I turned up hoping and praying that it would do the Peaky Blinders universe the justice it deserved when it made its daring move to the big screen, and it did not disappoint in any way. A finale worthy of ending a story I have personally adored all these years. We spent the first half of the movie flicking between how the events of the past have left Tommy withdrawn from the world, living in a house filled with the ghosts of all the people who he has loved but ultimately driven to their graves with his own ambitions. And then switching to see the future of the Peaky Blinders in the dangerous hands of his eldest son Duke, who's cool and peaceful way of dealing with violence is displayed with class through the slick performance of Barry Keoghan. The events that unfold are a mixture of slow methodical acting and high pace explosive action culminating in the shocking death of Ada. I think my mouth was left open for the next 5 minutes after this scene. This then seemed to propel the movie into what felt like part 2. And part 2... Classic Blinders!!! Heart racing shoot outs, a sound track that fit each and every scene to perfection and the pulsating story line which you just couldn't say for definite which way it was going to conclude. I had my feelings tho. Something inside was telling me that this is it, strap in and prepare for worst possible conclusion. The final scenes were pulsating. Filled with slow mo walks, mental flash backs to all the demons that have haunted Tommy all the way from Season 1 and the tunnels in France and striking music with breathtaking stress that only the blinders can put you through. And to finish!? Possibly the cruelest but kindest finish for our hero who has conquered every challenge put forward to him up until this point. In the end he left us on his own terms. Choosing death over life and making sure the people who matter most saw who he was handing his crown too. The new king of the gypsies. Duke! I tried my hardest through the final scenes in the field to convince myself that this is just television and shouldn't affect me in a way that it was. But these people, this family they've been the only thing I have ever felt this way about in the world of entertainment. Ask me my favourite band or song or anything really and I couldn't give you an answer. But ask me which show has stuck me with from beginning to end and will do after tonight and it will only ever be the Peaky Blinders. I guess with that being said this review could be seen as biased. Be that as it may, I can only tell you how the movie made me feel and it made me feel like someone who means something to me is no longer in this world (as much as a fictional person could be in my world) For me, it was perfect. Even the death of Ada makes sense now. It's a complete finale. The family we grew up with are now all gone. All that's left are the future of the Blinders, which seems to be in good hands after the performance of keoghan. Thank you Steven Knight for bringing this story into our lives. Thank you for always believing in it and making us believe in it. I will of course watch the whole saga again and then maybe again over time. Only now, every scene will mean so much more as we finally know who the only man that could kill Tommy Shelby was Tommy Shelby himself.

raw_payload.author: sisnade
raw_payload.content: I enjoyed it, it had all the style and tension that you come to expect from Peaky Blinders, but it was just missing something and I can't put my finger on it! Barry Keoghan's Duke will be a great successor to Tommy if he stays on for the new series. Tim Roth as a Nazi is an interesting choice.. it sort of worked. I think this is one of those film that would of benefited from more call backs and maybe leaning on the extended universe it spent years creating rather than just relying on a few key characters.

The cast are fantastic and the script is amazing

Hurry and get writing mr knight

Bravo.

raw_payload.author: Jh5wk
raw_payload.content:
Extremely underwhelming. While the film does a great job at keeping the Peaky experience alive, it was best said when there is a difference between adding depth and substance and extending a story.

SPOILER ALERT:

The plot was set prior to the killing of Ada (which by the way was completely unnecessary); however the entire plot was hijacked turning it into a revenge plot for her death. The WW2 plot line was not explored after extremely high hopes - in fact there ended up being no real plot line at all.

The idea that Duke was "way worse" than Tommy was touched on in the opening scene ultimately followed up by zero credit or backing of his "terrible" character.

Next - Tommy would NEVER kill Arthur. Should have left it at an accident in a drunken stupor or fight OR left it at a believable suicide.

The ending was always what we were going to get. It was the one saving grace of the film and wrapped up the unbelievable character of Tommy Shelby beautifully. The only issue I had with it were the fact that Polly always said a bullet will never kill Tommy Shelby.

Overall a perfect example of leaving a great thing alone and not forcing it further at the desire of the fans - ESPECIALLY in the form of a movie. In my mind Tommy forever rode off on his white horse at the end of the series and even though the highly anticipated plot line of WW2 was never explored, I can live with that prior ending and sleep peacefully. In the bleak midwinter.

raw_payload.author: nigelbartman
raw_payload.content: As a fan, I was desperate to see the movie. I thought it would go to a higher level, which I know was asking a lot. Sadly the plot ran its inevitable course. Cillian Murphy has amazing screen presence, and his return to the Garrison is memorable. But I was left wondering what if? Any fan will savour the movie, but a missed opportunity to take characters to a new level.
